title: Information Technology Lesson Plan - psuedo code
subject: Information Technology

data.title: Information Technology Lesson Plan - psuedo code

objectives.text: Create solutions using productivity tools, databases, and web technologies.
Design and implement simple algorithms or programs to solve problems.
Interpret user needs and select appropriate technological solutions.

lesson.subject: Information Technology
objectives: Design and implement simple algorithms or programs to solve problems.
Interpret user needs and select appropriate technological solutions.
Create solutions using productivity tools, databases, and web technologies.
prior_knowledge_questions: What is an algorithm in simple terms?
Name three control structures (sequence, selection, iteration).
How does a flowchart relate to writing psuedo code?
resources: Information Technology textbook section on psuedo code
Printed worksheet with 2 short problem prompts
YouTube title suggestion: psuedo code explained for Information Technology

Engagement: Teacher presents a short real-life scenario (e.g., making a class attendance list and marking late students) and asks students to list the steps verbally; record steps on the board in sequence.
Students call out step-by-step actions, identify missing details (inputs, outputs, conditions), and vote on the clearest sequence.
Exploration: Teacher gives each pair a worksheet with two tasks (e.g., calculate average grade; determine pass/fail based on threshold) and explains simple psuedo code conventions to use (INPUT, PROCESS, OUTPUT, IF, FOR/WHILE).
Students work in pairs for 10 minutes to write plain-language steps then convert them into structured psuedo code using the conventions; prepare one solution to present.
Explanation: Teacher invites 2–3 pairs to present their psuedo code on the board, highlights correct use of variables, control structures, and handling of edge cases; ask targeted questions to probe reasoning.
Students explain why they chose particular structures, respond to teacher prompts about user needs (e.g., what if no input provided), and update their psuedo code when given suggestions.
Evaluation: Teacher issues a 7-minute individual exit task: write psuedo code to find the largest of five numbers; provide a short rubric (correct logic, clear steps, use of control structures) and collect responses.
Students complete the exit task independently, hand it in, and if time remains, quickly peer-check a neighbour’s solution for clarity against the rubric.
Extension: Teacher assigns a take-home mini-project: design psuedo code for a simple database search (e.g., find student by ID) to be submitted via shared document next lesson; include required inputs/outputs and one test case.
Students outline inputs, expected outputs, and write psuedo code; they walk through the logic with a sample record to ensure the solution meets the user need.

assessment: Collected exit task: graded against rubric for correct logic, appropriate control structures, and clarity.
Pair presentation contributions and group discussion participation recorded informally.
Take-home mini-project reviewed for ability to interpret user needs and produce a workable psuedo code solution.
reflection: Note which control structures students used confidently and which required re-teaching.
Assess whether students linked user needs to required inputs/outputs effectively.
Decide if more practice with edge cases or converting psuedo code to code is needed next lesson.
Record any resource or timing changes (e.g., more time for pair work or clearer worksheet prompts).

title: Language Arts Lesson Plan - singular and plural
subject: Language Arts

data.title: Language Arts Lesson Plan - singular and plural

lesson.subject: Language Arts
prior_knowledge_questions: Can you name one toy you have?
Do you know a word for more than one cat?
How many apples are here? (show 1 vs 3)
resources: Language Arts textbook section on singular and plural
Picture/word cards showing one item and many of the same item
Small counters or cubes for counting

Engagement: Teacher: Hold up two picture cards (one dog, three dogs) and ask, “Which is one? Which is many?” Students: Point to the correct card and say “one” or “many.”
Teacher: Lead a short clap chant (clap once for one, clap three times for many). Students: Repeat the chant and clap the correct number.
Exploration: Teacher: Give each pair of students a mixed set of picture cards and two baskets labelled ONE and MANY. Students: Work in pairs to sort cards into the baskets.
Teacher: Move around the room, ask each pair to name one card from each basket aloud. Students: Say the singular and the plural word for chosen cards.
Explanation: Teacher: Write three examples on the board (cat → cats, book → books, box → boxes) and point out the added letter(s). Students: Read the examples aloud with the teacher.
Teacher: Explain simply: for most words add -s for more than one; show one -es example (box → boxes). Students: Change three of their sorted singular cards into plurals and say them aloud.
Evaluation: Teacher: Give a short worksheet with 6 items: circle the plural pictures and write the plural form for three nouns. Students: Complete the worksheet independently.
Teacher: Use a quick exit check — each student shows thumb up if they can make plurals, thumb side if unsure, thumb down if they need help. Students: Show the appropriate thumb.
Extension: Teacher: Assign a home drawing: draw one item and then draw three of the same; write the words under each drawing. Students: Take the sheet home and complete with a family member.
Teacher: Tell students to listen for plural words during story time tomorrow and bring one example to share. Students: Listen and bring one plural word to class.

assessment: Mark the worksheet: correct identification of plurals and correct written plural forms (3 written items).
Use the exit-thumb responses and notes from circulation to identify 3 students needing extra support.
Ask each student to orally give the plural for one pictured item before leaving (quick one-word check).
reflection: Note which examples caused errors (e.g., words needing -es) and plan one extra practice activity for those.
Record pacing: did students finish the worksheet in the given time or need extension?
Reflect on student engagement during sorting — which pairs needed more teacher prompts?
Decide next lesson focus (more practice with irregulars or more -s/-es examples) based on assessment results.
